nativeRange.habitat: Sluggish tributaries, small rivers, and swampy blackwater zones with dense aquatic vegetation, submerged woody debris, and leaf litter; typically stained, tannin-rich water with very low mineral content

decorPreferences: heavily planted with fine-leaved plants (Java moss, floating plants)
dark substrate to reduce stress and enhance coloration
dried Indian almond (catappa) leaves for tannins and microbe colonies

diet.notes: Micro-predator in nature, feeding on tiny invertebrates and zooplankton. In the aquarium, offer high-quality small dried foods (micro pellets, crushed flakes) alongside daily portions of live or frozen micro-foods: Artemia nauplii, Daphnia, Moina, micro bloodworms, white mosquito larvae, and grindal worms. Small mouth size demands appropriately sized prey; avoid large frozen or dry foods.

goodWith: small peaceful characins (ember tetras, neon tetras)
dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma spp.) as dither fish
avoidWith: large or boisterous fish that may outcompete at feeding
cherry shrimp and dwarf shrimp (predation risk from adults)
fast-moving fish that cause stress in timid shoals

imageCandidates.notes: Male specimen; photographer credited as C. Appleby in FishBase contributor records.

summary: The One-Lined Pencilfish is a slender 5 cm Amazonian characin distinguished by a single bold black mid-lateral stripe and a unique oblique swim posture — head angled upward ~45°, unlike most pencilfish species. Best kept in soft, acidic blackwater in groups of 8+; an intermediate species due to strict water chemistry requirements.
careNotes:
Nannostomus unifasciatus is one of only two Nannostomus species that adopts a pronounced oblique, snout-up swimming posture — angling the body at roughly 45° with the head toward the surface — setting it apart visually from the horizontal resting posture of most pencilfish. A single bold black lateral stripe runs the full body length onto the caudal fin, bordered above by a narrow gold or copper iridescent band. Like all pencilfish, it switches to a cryptic vertically-barred night coloration, a hardwired diurnal rhythm that occurs even in blind specimens.

This species demands soft, acidic water in the blackwater tradition of the Amazon: target pH 5.0–6.5, gH under 8, and temperature 24–27°C for long-term health. Hard or alkaline tap water will suppress coloration and immune function; reverse osmosis water remineralized to very low hardness, with Indian almond leaves or driftwood for tannins, is strongly recommended. Gentle flow is essential — an air-powered sponge filter is ideal.

Keep in groups of at least 8; smaller groups produce timid, washed-out fish. A species tank or a carefully matched community with similarly small, soft-water species (ember tetras, Corydoras, Apistogramma) works well. The species occupies the upper-to-middle water column, making it an effective dither fish for dwarf cichlids.

Mouth size is small; all foods must be appropriately tiny. Artemia nauplii, Daphnia, Moina, and micro bloodworms are staples. High-quality micro pellets and crushed flakes are accepted once the fish have settled. Avoid strong surface agitation that disperses food before fish can reach it.

CareLevel is intermediate due to strict soft-acid water requirements and sensitivity to standard tap water chemistry.
breedingNotes: As of the time of research, Nannostomus unifasciatus is the only Nannostomus species in the aquarium trade for which no confirmed successful captive spawning has been documented, despite being kept in aquaria since at least the 1920s. The reasons are not fully understood; leading hypotheses focus on extreme water softness and acidity requirements possibly not replicated closely enough, or undiscovered environmental triggers. Related species (N. eques, N. beckfordi) spawn on fine-leaved plant surfaces or floating plants in very soft, acidic water with dim lighting, scattering small adhesive eggs; males may guard briefly. Any attempted breeding of N. unifasciatus should replicate extreme blackwater conditions (pH 4.5–5.5, gH < 2, temperature 26–28°C, tannin-stained water) with dense fine vegetation and minimal disturbance.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nannostomus unifasciatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided water parameters (pH 4.0–7.0, hardness 18–179 ppm, temperature 23–28°C), adult size (35–40 mm SL), tank dimensions (75 × 30 cm minimum), native range, habitat, diet, behavior (oblique swim posture, night coloration), compatibility, and the notable finding that no captive spawning has been recorded.

additional.name: FishBase — Nannostomus unifasciatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Lebiasinidae, Characiformes), maximum size (3.8 cm SL), temperature range (25–28°C), native distribution (Amazon basin of Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia; upper Orinoco basin of Venezuela; Guyana), and diet (worms, crustaceans, insects).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pencilfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General pencilfish husbandry guide covering temperature 24–27°C, pH 6.5–7.5, minimum tank 10–29 gal, diet (baby brine shrimp, cyclops, daphnia, crushed flakes), compatibility with corydoras/tetras/rasboras, and avoidance of shrimp. N. unifasciatus listed among covered species.

additional.name: Aquadiction — One-Lined Pencilfish Profile
additional.notes: Supplementary trade source. Confirmed oblique swim posture, single black lateral stripe with gold border, group size recommendation of 10+, soft acidic water preference, and compatible tankmates (tetras, corydoras, loricarids, killifish, Apistogramma, discus).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Nannostomus unifasciatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked etymology (uni = one, fasciatus = banded), species description history (Steindachner 1876), distribution including introduced Trinidad and Tobago population, and oblique posture shared with N. eques as one of only two Nannostomus species with this characteristic.

fish.breedingNotes: No confirmed captive spawning on record despite the species being kept in aquaria since the 1920s. Extreme soft-acid blackwater conditions (pH 4.5–5.5, gH < 2) should be replicated in any attempt. Related species spawn on fine plant surfaces; eggs are small and adhesive.
